case_name: Arksey v Cambridge University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust [2019] EWHC 1276 (QB) (21 March 2019)

evidence_index.text: The claimant, Mrs Margaret Arksey, seeks damages for alleged negligence arising out of the failure of the defendant hospital, Addenbrooke's in Cambridge, to admit her on 3 November 2012 after she had suffered a sentinel bleed from a cerebral aneurysm.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The defendant's case is that, although they were admittedly negligent in failing to admit Mrs Arksey to hospital and carry out a CT scan, this in fact made no difference because nothing that would or could have been done would have avoided the re-bleed the following day.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The claimant developed hydrocephalus and in May 2014 a ventricular peritoneal (VP) shunt was inserted to relieve intracranial pressure.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On 3 November 2012, an ambulance was called to the address of the Arksey family at 38 Cage Hill, Swaffham, Cambridgeshire, it being Mr and Mrs Arksey's son who summoned the ambulance.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The patient record form, or PRF, for that attendance by the East of England Ambulance Service is at pages 276-277 of the medical records bundle.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: 'My wife went upstairs to bed in the late afternoon/early evening and was in bed with her head on two pillows and she was not laid flat.'
evidence_index.speaker: Mr. Arksey

evidence_index.text: On Sunday 4 November, Mr Arksey recalls that their son, Joseph, found the claimant collapsed on the floor in the bathroom and had rung the emergency services, this being at about 7.30am.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The ambulance was on the scene at 10.04 and they found Mrs Arksey sat in her chair, complaining of a strange sensation in her left side.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: It appears to be agreed that this deterioration represented a significant re-bleed from the cerebral aneurysm in the form a major subarachnoid haemorrhage.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mr Trivedi provided his statement, to which I have already referred, in July 2018, to deal with the allegations of negligence effectively aimed at him or his team alleging a wider or longer delay than that which had been admitted by the defendant.
evidence_index.speaker: Mr. Trivedi

evidence_index.text: The consequence is that there will be judgment for the claimant only for the admitted breach of duty in relation to the failure to carry out the VP shunt for a period from 31 January 2014 to 14 May 2014, a period of three and a half months, but otherwise the claim fails.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient was found by her son in the bathroom, 'staring through him', complaining of neck ache. She had slurred speech, was confused as to her age and time, and appeared agitated.

focused_exam.neurological: Confused, slurred speech, Glasgow Coma Score of 14

requestables.name: CT Brain
requestables.description: CT scan of the brain to check for hemorrhage

reveal.result_summary: Not performed initially; later showed extensive subarachnoid hemorrhage
reveal.clinical_significance: Would have identified the aneurysm and bleeding
reveal.decision_impact: Should have prompted immediate neurosurgical intervention

timeline_phases.name: Emergency Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to ED with acute symptoms

timeline_phases.name: Initial Workup
timeline_phases.description: Initial assessment and decision-making in ED

key_events.event: Initial assessment and vital signs recorded

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in ED with symptoms suggestive of subarachnoid hemorrhage
decision_points.prompt: What imaging investigation should be ordered?

options.clinical_reasoning: Standard investigation for suspected subarachnoid hemorrhage

expected_action_court.description: CT brain should have been ordered
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established CT is standard for suspected subarachnoid hemorrhage

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to order CT scan led to missed diagnosis of aneurysm
explanation.what_should_have_happened: CT scan would have identified the aneurysm and allowed for timely intervention

patient_outcome.description: Patient suffered a major re-bleed leading to significant disability
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Wheelchair-bound, cognitive impairment, unable to return to work

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to order CT scan
Causation established - earlier diagnosis would have prevented re-bleed

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: CT would have shown aneurysm, allowing for intervention
